summary: Tractate Yoma daf 79b covers: Egg-bulk measure for YK; how eating measures were determined; combining foods.

display.hint: The Gemara discusses the egg-bulk (beitza) measure used in many halachic contexts, and how it relates to the YK eating measure. The discussion covers how the Sages determined these measures and whethe...
display.title: Egg-Bulk and Date-Bulk: Calibrating Yom Kippur Measures

learning.ahaMoment: The Talmud's system of measures is applied physical science: each measure is derived from something stable and observable, and the egg is the most universally reliable calibration point.
learning.memoryAnchor: Egg-bulk is the baseline: stable, uniform, universally available - all measures calibrate against it.
learning.learnerQuestion: How does the egg-bulk (beitza) measure relate to the Yom Kippur eating prohibition?
learning.coreTension: Between the various halakhic measures used in different contexts (olive-bulk, egg-bulk, date-bulk) and the need to understand how they relate to each other and to a shared physical referent.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara anchors halakhic measures to physical realities that can be observed and calibrated. The egg-bulk serves as a baseline because eggs are uniform and universally available; from this stable baseline, other measures can be derived or compared.

takeaway.text: Halakhic measures are not arbitrary but are anchored to stable physical referents; the egg-bulk serves as a baseline because of its uniformity and universal availability, making it the most reliable calibration standard.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may find the multiple measures (olive, egg, date) confusing; recognizing them as a coherent physical calibration system makes each measure's rationale clear.

argumentFlow.label: The pit makes a large date exceed an egg
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara resolves the tension: although two pitless dates do not equal an egg, a single large date with its pit does exceed an egg-bulk, because date pits are very large. Rav Pappa cites the folk saying that two kav of dates yield more than a kav of pits, confirming how large the pits are.

argumentFlow.label: Rava: fruit simply need not be eaten in a sukka
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rava dismisses the whole proof: the dates could be eaten outside the sukka because fruit never requires a sukka. An objection from Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi eating figs and grapes outside the sukka is reread to mean fruit is always treated as a casual snack, not that a fixed fruit meal needs a sukka.

argumentFlow.label: An alternative reading of the fruit meal
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Alternatively, the Gemara explains that the scholars ate the fruit as a fixed meal but ate bread with it only casually, to temper the fruit's sweetness, and that bread-eating was done outside the sukka, which is why the episode does not prove fruit requires a sukka.

argumentFlow.label: Does the 'sweets' baraita support Rava?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: A baraita that one may complete the sukka-meal requirement with 'sweets' seems to support Rava, since it does not say 'fruit.' The Gemara rejects the proof: 'sweets' here means fruit, or the baraita speaks of a place where fruit is scarce, so no support can be drawn.

argumentFlow.label: Rav Zevid: the large date is smaller than an egg
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rav Zevid reverses course, holding the large date is less than an egg-bulk, from the mishna where Beit Shammai set the measure of forbidden leavened bread on Passover at the volume of a large date, a measure they sought one size above an olive-bulk.

argumentFlow.label: Why Beit Shammai needed two distinct measures
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara explains Beit Shammai's reasoning: had leaven and leavened bread shared a measure, the Torah need not have written both. Since strong leaven is forbidden at an olive-bulk, weaker leavened bread, mentioned separately, must carry a larger measure, namely the volume of a large date.

argumentFlow.label: The large date is the size just above an olive
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara argues that since Beit Shammai sought the measure one size larger than an olive-bulk, and did not simply say 'egg,' the large date must be smaller than an egg. Had it equaled an egg they would have used the more common egg measure, so it concludes the large date is less than an egg.

argumentFlow.label: Rejecting the proof from Beit Shammai
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara rejects that inference: perhaps a large date does exceed an egg while a normal-sized date equals an egg, and Beit Shammai meant a normal date; or a large date and an egg are equal and the mishna simply chose one term. Either way no proof emerges from Beit Shammai.

argumentFlow.label: Proof from the zimmun dispute over satisfaction
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: A firmer proof: Rabbi Meir requires an olive-bulk to obligate zimmun, Rabbi Yehuda an egg-bulk, disputing whether 'eat and be satisfied' is defined by eating (olive) or by satisfying eating (egg). The egg-bulk is thus the threshold of satisfaction.

argumentFlow.label: Therefore the large date is less than an egg
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Since an egg-bulk is the measure that satisfies, were the large date larger than an egg it would already satisfy, yet eating a large date only 'settles the mind' without removing affliction's deeper relief. Hence the large date must be less than an egg: it settles the mind but does not fully satisfy.

argumentFlow.label: Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi on Torah measures
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara introduces Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi's teaching, which the discussion of Torah measures continues onto the next page, that the standard eating-measures of the Torah are an olive-bulk except where the verse's language signals otherwise.

theological: The egg-bulk is a practical measure from daily life - the Rabbis anchored abstra
narrative: Egg-bulk measure for YK; how eating measures were determined

requiresUnderstanding: Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Learners think the size of the large date is a fixed fact rather than a contested derivation.
misconceptions.correction: The Gemara records a genuine dispute: some hold the large date exceeds an egg (because of the huge pit), Rav Zevid holds it is smaller, and the matter is argued from the sukka episodes, Beit Shammai's chametz measure, and the zimmun debate before concluding it is less than an egg.

quizSeeds.question: How does the Gemara reconcile that two pitless dates are smaller than an egg yet a large date with its pit exceeds one?
quizSeeds.answer: The pit accounts for the difference. Date pits are unusually large, as the folk saying notes that two kav of dates contain over a kav of pits, so the flesh of two dates falls short of an egg while a single date counted with its very large pit surpasses it.

quizSeeds.question: How does the zimmun dispute help fix the size of the large date relative to an egg?
quizSeeds.answer: Rabbi Yehuda holds an egg-bulk is the measure of satisfying eating. Since a large date only 'settles the mind' rather than fully satisfies, it must be less than an egg; were it larger it would already satisfy and remove the affliction outright.

rashiTranslations.he: הא כביצה בעי סוכה. אלמא שתי כותבות דאמרן לעיל לא שהלכה כן
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: the egg-bulk as a general Torah measure for eating.

rashiTranslations.he: פחותות הן מכביצה: שתי כותבות בלא גרעינן. שאכלן רבן
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: how various foods' amounts are calculated toward the eating measure.

rashiTranslations.he: גמליאל בסוכה ואמרו עלייהו לא שהלכה כן: תרי קבי דתמרי.
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: combining different types of food toward the eating minimum.

rashiTranslations.he: בגרעינהון הוו קבא דקשייתא: וסריח. יש בהן קב גרעינין ויותר
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: the relationship between the egg-bulk and other Torah measures.

by; and if a date is less than an egg-bulk,

rashiTranslations.he: ליתני כביצה ואי נמי כי הדדי
rashiTranslations.en: let the mishna teach 'egg-bulk' instead. And even if they are equal,
let it say 'egg-bulk,' which is the more common measure
in the mishna - more so than 'date-bulk' - regarding
the impurity of foods. 'A date plain means an egg-bulk' -
Rashi: and Beit Shammai hold 'a date plain' is what was stated.
Rashi: 'mezamnin' - they recite the grace over food after it.
